content.abstract_or_snippet: atability within the simulated environment, validating the feasibility and effectiveness of zero on-site testing for GNSS-based train control systems. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways. 1. Introduction Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) have seen vast applications in the transportation domain. The integration of Global Positioning System (GPS) and Beidou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) in railway is efficient for modernizing the railway network, GNSS is increasingly becoming a fundamental pillar not only for non-safety applications, but also for im- proved maintenance, automated train operation, and safety- related appli- cations, including the Next Generation of Train Control (NGTC) and re- novation of the train on-board equipment, all these will increase the railway system capacity, efficiency and safety of all railways [1]. To apply GNSS in railway train operation, the GNSS performance testing and verification is the essential procedure for the system to be adopted and further listed as mandatory in the system design and development. The certification of the GNSS-based train localization systems is also necessary to provide concrete validation or testing application services to carry out simulation tests with evaluation of the platform and environmental condi- tion simulations [2]. Beidou has a set of technical and regulatory standards, including the BDS Performance Standard, BDS Signal Interface Control Document (ICD), and technical requirements for user equipment. The BDS Signal In Space (SIS) and service performance characteristics include ac- curacy, availability, compatibility, and interoperability [3]. GPS has also published standard positioning service performance standards to guide the user for civil uses worldwide, the performance metrics are GPS SIS accuracy, continuity, and availability [4]. So both BDS and GPS are focusing on ac- curacy as the foundation for the GNSS performance [5]. The performance needs to be assessed and as the performances are all credited to the equipment, the equipment itself needs to be certified. The certification procedure, it is governed by various international and regional standards, such as the accuracy standards by BDS and GPS, the test and simulation standard procedures, the simulation must meet possible (as sensible to the GNSS) to make the test results plausible. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con- ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor- mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re- latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work. GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi- tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys- tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa- tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ- ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the anti- jamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-j

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning Zhuojian Caoa, Jiang Liua,b,c,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Wei Jianga,b,c, Baigen Caia,b,c ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS jamming Deeply-coupled integration GNSS/INS Railway safety and efficiency increasingly rely on precise train positioning. The integration of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) into railway control systems aims to reduce dependence on track-side in- frastructure. While GNSS has significantly improved train localization, challenges such as the susceptibility to jamming remain. To address this, this paper introduces an Inertial Navigation System (INS)-aided train posi- tioning system based on deep integration, exploring its performance through semi-physical experiments and simulations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed solution is able to reduce the positioning error by 63.47 %, and the velocity error by 58.47 % under jamming conditions. The study highlights the potential of deep integration for improving the resilience of GNSS-based train control systems, especially in the face of Radio Frequency (RF) jamming threats. 1. Introduction Railway safety and efficiency depend increasingly on knowing every train’s position. Traditional train positioning technologies usually in- volve track circuit positioning, axle counter positioning, and intersec- tion inductive loop positioning. However, these methods are often limited by insufficient positioning accuracy and high maintenance costs. The application of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) in railway train control systems aims to reduce as much as possible the use of track-side infrastructures for train location awareness, including track circuits and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. Jiang et al. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 It is of great importance to develop specific countermeasures to protect train positioning from jamming-caused GNSS disturbance. Research institutions and GNSS receiver manufacturers have been developing GNSS receivers equipped with anti-jamming capabilities. A survey of state-of-art approaches is introduced, based on the functional stages of the receiver in which they operate. Typically, they could be implemented at the front-end, pre-correction, or post-correlation stages, with others functioning at the navigation processing stage. Spatial fil- tering [9–11], functioning at the front-end stage, uses antenna arrays to point the receiver antenna beam toward the GNSS satellites and away from jammers. In the pre-correction stage, time/frequency filtering [12–14] enables the jamming mitigation before the jamming signal enters the receiver. Vector tracking is a typical approach that works on the post-correction stage. Instead of tracking in-view satellites sepa- rately in the conventional baseband signal processing, vector tracking [15,16] is achieved through feedback of the receiver states from the navigation module to the signal tracking channels as prior information and constraints. Additionally, the inertial-aiding-based strategy at the navigation processing stage provides a different way. INS is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals,

High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 and the Doppler radar, also show great potentials in realizing the au- tonomous train positioning with a low cost over the Balise-based solu- tions [4]. However, it can be found that the single-sensor-based solution cannot satisfy the requirement of the train control system due to specific limitations and disadvantages in practical scenarios [5]. How to explore the capabilities of multiple perception sensors and break through the application-demand-oriented information fusion processing technology is the key to realize the satisfaction of novel train control systems with the train-centric design. Hence, it is important to overcome the key technologies of autonomous train positioning based on the fusion of GNSS and assistant sensors. By developing a novel train control system with dynamic configuration of the trains’ interval, reduced involvement of track-side equipments, and enhanced maintainability of the on-board train positioning system, the advantages of the train-centric mode will be practically reflected and utilized for specific railway lines. In this paper, key technologies of the novel train control system with GNSS- based autonomous perception are investigated and demonstrated in the practical environment, which illustrate the necessity and advantages of the utilization of GNSS, especially BDS (BeiDou Navigation Satellite System), in future safety-related railway systems in China. 2. Multi-sensor data fusion In existing railway signaling systems, the track occupancy of the train is determined by the track circuit, and the exact along-track lo- cation of the train is calculated by integrating both the train-borne odometer (or Doppler radar) and track-side Balises. The novel train- centric signaling system determines the track occupancy and train lo- cation without relying on those track-side facilities. Thus, raw mea- surements from multiple train-borne sensors will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. GNSS/INS/Odometer sensor data fusion The ODO (Odometer) is the most commonly used train positioning method in existing train control systems. Wheel slide and slip during the train operation, as well as wheel diameter wear, may result in errors in the train speed measurement, and lead to error accumulation in the along-track location calculation results. Such errors will be brought into the INS/ODO integrated positioning system. The train positioning error by INS/ODO drifts over time until the position calibration results are available. Different from the Balises, results of GNSS positioning can provide the correction without relying on the track-side. Performance of a GNSS/INS/ODO integrated positioning system will be determined by the reliability and accuracy of GNSS. When the GNSS performance decreases or the GNSS signal is blocked for a long time, the result of INS will not be guaranteed effectively [6]. Therefore, the availability and observation quality of GNSS greatly affect the precision and effective- ness of train positioning using INS and ODO. The key issue in realizing the multi-sensor integrated train positioning is the sensor fusion logic. It is in charge of combining the measurements from multiple sensors, improving the reliability and fault tolerance of the integrated system, providing suitable auxiliary information for the ex- isting location determination system, and simplifying the system com- plexity over those track-side-relied solutions. Among different sensor fu- sion algorithms, Federated Kalman Filter (FKF) is a distributed cascaded integrated sensor fusion method and widely considered in applications because of its flexibility, low computational effort, and excellent fault tolerance capability. FKF consists of several local filters and a main filter, in which the system information is allocated in the local filters and then fused in the main filter. For the GNSS/INS/ODO integrated train posi- tioning system based on the FKF, INS is integrated due to its advantages in high output rate, autonomy and reliability. Considering the INS-related local filtering structures, two local filters for GNSS/INS and INS/ODO can 48 Fig. 1. Architecture of GNSS/INS/ODO sensor data fusion for integrated train positioning. be established, as shown in Fig. 1. Based on this traditional tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integra- tion, the estimated navigation states are used to estimate the Doppler shift information, which is fed back to the GNSS tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The system operates in a closed-loop way where the integrated filter continuously updates the system’s state estimation by combining data from both the GNSS and INS, and the feedback of the estimated states helps improve the accuracy of both the INS and GNSS calculations. The INS-aided deep integration system leverages the in- ertial data, effectively narrowing the bandwidth of the tracking loop, which helps reduce the noise within the loop and suppress external jamming signals. The system’s capability to maintain accurate tracking and position estimation in jamming environments is significantly strengthened, leading to a more resilient navigation solution. 3. Jamming analysis and protection 3.1. Jamming analysis The received signal strength of the GNSS receiver is extremely low and falls well below the thermal noise floor. Typically, the received GPS signal levels fall within the range of −127 dBm [22]. The weak char- acteristics of GNSS signals will lead to an increasing risk of performance degradation under jamming conditions. Jamming signals may emanate from telecommunication and electronic systems operating in adjacent bands or in bands relatively far from the GNSS bands, i.e., Fre- quency Modulation (FM)/TV transmitter harmonics, Amplitude Mod- ulation (AM) transmitters, and mobile phone networks. Through the integration of static trackmap information, sensor data fusion for BDS/GPS re- ceivers, INS sensors and the odometer measurements were real- timely achieved with a high availability level under the complex and time-changing operation environment. Both the static and dynamic test cases were involved in the field demonstration. Table 6 sum- marizes the accuracy results of train positioning under different system modes. The results from field demonstration illustrate that involvement of the presented key technologies, including the safe platform and multi- source sensor fusion mechanism, effectively guarantees the perfor- mance of GNSS-based autonomous train positioning and state percep- tion even under complex and time-varying operational conditions. The utilization of the differential GNSS technology and the integrity mon- itoring logic makes it possible of achieving the high accuracy and ro- bustness against the possible difficult or even critical conditions. The field test results demonstrate the feasibility and the advantages of utilizing BDS in realizing a novel autonomous train control system with a train-centric design, which is of great significance in the future high- speed and plateau railway applications with the enhanced environ- mental flexibility and whole life cycle cost efficiency. 7. Conclusion The application of GNSS technology is one of the representative characteristics of intelligent railway systems in the future. However, there are specific requirements to the GNSS-based train positioning and state perception in the railway signaling applications due to the critical re- quirement to the safety. Thus, the utilization of GNSS has to be enhanced by the specific technical solutions. The sensor data fusion technique is of great significance to enhance the service availability under degraded or even a critical GNSS observing condition. To ensure the compatibility to the existing system specifications, the VB technology enables the in- tegration of the GNSS capabilities into the train control system and meanwhile the train integrity monitoring logic allows the opportunity to realize moving block-based train operation control through the dynamic identification of the track occupancy and safe envelope of the train. By investigating the relationship between the GNSS performance system and the RAMS specification, the performance evaluation of the GNSS-based train positioning can be effectively realized. It can be found that the in- volvement of these key technologies consolidates the autonomy and cap- abilities of the train-borne system, which releases the requirement to the track-side facilities like track circuit and Balises. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Fig. 8. The positioning errors and the velocity errors for the normal tracking solution and the INS-aided solution in scenario II. 5. Conclusion In this paper, a GNSS jamming protection solution is presented based on the INS-aided deep integration solution. The solution enables a self-contained PVT reference that is immune to RF signals. INS in- formation is sent to the receiver’s baseband, realizing the assistance to the receiver at the signal processing level. The measured velocity in- formation from INS is mapped to the LOS between the satellite and the vehicle to obtain the Doppler of relative motion. The Doppler is fed back to the NCO in the tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The the proposed solution demonstrates significant evaluation of improvements in positioning and velocity, enabling resilient train po- sitioning for securing next generation GNSS-based train control sys- tems. Future work will focus on refining mitigation techniques with extended studies for multi-constellation scenarios (e.g., BeiDou and Galileo), and exploring field tests in real lines to ensure the resilience of GNSS-based train localization systems in railway applications. The main contributions of this paper are as follows. 1) A novel CPN-based modeling framework for GNSS-based train po- sitioning systems is developed. 2) Dynamic influences of signal interferences (i.e., by AM signals, FM signals, pulse signals) and environmental obstructions (i.e., by mountains and tunnels) are incorporated into the model. 3) The performance of the GNSS-based train positioning system is quantitatively evaluated under various scenarios with the CPN model of the system. 2. Related works Over the past decades, GNSS-based systems have been extensively ap- plied to train positioning due to their global coverage and high accuracy under favorable conditions [1,7]. Despite these advantages, challenges such as signal interferences and terrain-induced occlusions continue to pose questions on high positioning accuracy and stability. Numerous studies have explored techniques to mitigate the impacts of signal interferences on GNSS- based positioning accuracy [8–12]. For example, Ref. [9] investigates the impact of continuous wave interference on GNSS receiver tracking perfor- mance, examining the extent of its influence by varying data bit durations or integration times. To address interference-related challenges, an inter- ference energy detection method and a comprehensive orientation tech- nology tailored for GNSS are introduced in Ref. [11]. Besides, a number of researches have examined the impact of environmental factors along railway lines on GNSS signal integrity. For instance, Ref. [13] explores methods of scenario-based weighted measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. Some studies have achieved high positioning accuracy in partially obstructed or challenging environ- ments by fusing multi-sensor data [6,

Jiang et al. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 It is of great importance to develop specific countermeasures to protect train positioning from jamming-caused GNSS disturbance. Research institutions and GNSS receiver manufacturers have been developing GNSS receivers equipped with anti-jamming capabilities. A survey of state-of-art approaches is introduced, based on the functional stages of the receiver in which they operate. Typically, they could be implemented at the front-end, pre-correction, or post-correlation stages, with others functioning at the navigation processing stage. Spatial fil- tering [9–11], functioning at the front-end stage, uses antenna arrays to point the receiver antenna beam toward the GNSS satellites and away from jammers. In the pre-correction stage, time/frequency filtering [12–14] enables the jamming mitigation before the jamming signal enters the receiver. Vector tracking is a typical approach that works on the post-correction stage. Instead of tracking in-view satellites sepa- rately in the conventional baseband signal processing, vector tracking [15,16] is achieved through feedback of the receiver states from the navigation module to the signal tracking channels as prior information and constraints. Additionally, the inertial-aiding-based strategy at the navigation processing stage provides a different way. INS is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals,

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: terrain, with the elevation direction being particularly sensitive to these factors. Tunnel scenarios show the most pronounced degradation with a mean error of 5.6670 m. This dramatic increase results from severe signal blockages in tunnels and the instability during signal reacquisition after exiting the tunnel. Fig. 10 illustrates the evolution of position error in the tunnel en- vironment. During the “Inside the Tunnel” phase, GNSS signals are completely blocked by the surrounding structure, resulting in a loss of positioning capability (represented by the gray shaded region). Upon exiting the tunnel (marked by the vertical dashed line), the system begins to reacquire GNSS signals; however, due to severe multipath effects and signal obstructions, the initial position errors are sig- nificantly large. As the train moves further away from the tunnel exit, the surrounding environment improves, leading to a gradual reduction in error, which eventually stabilizes. This trend highlights the critical Fig. 10. Position errors in tunnel scenario. impact of tunnel environments and their transitions on GNSS-based positioning performance. 5. Conclusion This paper presents a CPNs-based approach to evaluate the perfor- mances of GNSS-based train positioning systems under real-world op- erational conditions. By constructing a hierarchical CPN model, the study successfully models the process of GNSS signal reception and interferences, as well as the impacts of complex environmental factors on the GNSS-based train positioning. The simulation results emphasize that signal interferences (i.e., interfered by AM, FM, and pulse signals) have a significant impact on the GNSS-based positioning accuracy, with FM interference exerting the most pronounced effects, particularly in the vertical direction. Environmental factors like terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding further degrade signal visibility and increase po- sitioning errors. The model also identifies critical challenges during signal reacquisition, particularly in tunnel transitions, which exacerbate error growth. While the proposed approach effectively demonstrates the impact of various signal interferences and environmental conditions on the per- formance of the GNSS-based train positioning, future research could explore the integration of additional signal interference mitigation techniques to improve the accuracy and stability of the GNSS-based positioning for railway applications. content.abstract_or_snippet: a signal jamming test environment. The setup of the GNSS jamming test platform is shown in Fig. 4, consisting of several key components, each connected through specific signal transmission paths, designed to enable jamming data collection for analytical processing. The Spirent GSS8000 GNSS simulator and the sight N5172B jamming signal generator are con- trolled from the simulator’s SimGEN host controller, which generates the required GPS L1 RF signals and the jamming signals, respectively. The jamming signals are defined, along with all the other scenario parameters, within the SimGEN. The scenario trajectory collected in the field is input into the SimGEN software, driving the simulator to generate the corre- sponding dynamic GNSS RF signals. A signal combiner is utilized to in- tegrate the clean GPS RF signal and the specific jamming signal. Since the down-converted Intermediate Frequency (IF) signal is what we need, the HG-softGPS06 GNSS IF signal collector is connected to the simulated signal to capture the necessary data, which is then collected and post-processed by the PC host. Field test results from a train location system were collected and used as the scenario script to drive the controller. A tactical-grade SPAN system (a high-precision GNSS/INS integrated system with a tactical grade, low noise IMU-FSAS) installed on the train was utilized to collect the tra- jectory and the IMU data. Two practical local trajectories that last for 160 s are adopted as the operation script to generate different GNSS jamming tested scenarios and drive the GNSS simulator. The key parameters of the trajectory are listed as follows. Scenario I: The trajectory starts at GPS week 2034, second of week 288888, lasting for 160 s. Based on this traditional tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integra- tion, the estimated navigation states are used to estimate the Doppler shift information, which is fed back to the GNSS tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The system operates in a closed-loop way where the integrated filter continuously updates the system’s state estimation by combining data from both the GNSS and INS, and the feedback of the estimated states helps improve the accuracy of both the INS and GNSS calculations. The INS-aided deep integration system leverages the in- ertial data, effectively narrowing the bandwidth of the tracking loop, which helps reduce the noise within the loop and suppress external jamming signals. The system’s capability to maintain accurate tracking and position estimation in jamming environments is significantly strengthened, leading to a more resilient navigation solution. 3. Jamming analysis and protection 3.1. Jamming analysis The received signal strength of the GNSS receiver is extremely low and falls well below the thermal noise floor. Typically, the received GPS signal levels fall within the range of −127 dBm [22]. The weak char- acteristics of GNSS signals will lead to an increasing risk of performance degradation under jamming conditions. Jamming signals may emanate from telecommunication and electronic systems operating in adjacent bands or in bands relatively far from the GNSS bands, i.e., Fre- quency Modulation (FM)/TV transmitter harmonics, Amplitude Mod- ulation (AM) transmitters, and mobile phone networks. [23–25], based on the time-frequency behavior, jamming signals can be typically divided into several classes, including Continuous Wave (CW), AM, FM, Frequency Sweep (FS; also referred to is the sampling interval, and as chirp jamming), Narrow-Band Gaussian white Noise (NBGN), and Wideband Gaussian white Noise (WBGN). It is found in Ref. [25] that the majority of the jammers use chirp signals, which vary the signal frequency linearly over time . The chirp signal exhibits a constant rate of frequency change over time, within a certain frequency range and sweep period. This sweep procedure is characterized by the initial and frequency , and the sweep period T. Thus, the instantaneous frequency is , the minimum and maximum frequency at given by The time-domain representation of the chirp signal is modeled as (2) (3) The jamming signals will not only disrupt the proper functioning of the receiver’s RF front-end components, such as low-noise amplifiers, automatic gain control, and analog-to-digital converters, but also ad- versely affect the operation of the carrier and code tracking loops. This jamming can prevent the receiver from acquiring and maintaining a stable lock on the satellite signals, ultimately leading to degraded po- sitioning performance. 3.2. INS-aided jamming protection solution Fig. 2 illustrates the INS-aided GNSS tracking loop. The carrier signal measurement enters the tracking loop through the phase dis- criminator to generate a phase error estimate. This phase error drives the carrier loop filter which outputs an estimate of the signal Doppler to drive the carrier controller oscillator (NCO). The NCO duplicates the phase of the incoming signal, and this phase estimate is fed back to the phase discriminator. The tracking loop is usually said to be “locked” if the phase error is less than five degrees. content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning Zhuojian Caoa, Jiang Liua,b,c,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Wei Jianga,b,c, Baigen Caia,b,c ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS jamming Deeply-coupled integration GNSS/INS Railway safety and efficiency increasingly rely on precise train positioning. The integration of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) into railway control systems aims to reduce dependence on track-side in- frastructure. While GNSS has significantly improved train localization, challenges such as the susceptibility to jamming remain. To address this, this paper introduces an Inertial Navigation System (INS)-aided train posi- tioning system based on deep integration, exploring its performance through semi-physical experiments and simulations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed solution is able to reduce the positioning error by 63.47 %, and the velocity error by 58.47 % under jamming conditions. The study highlights the potential of deep integration for improving the resilience of GNSS-based train control systems, especially in the face of Radio Frequency (RF) jamming threats. 1. Introduction Railway safety and efficiency depend increasingly on knowing every train’s position. Traditional train positioning technologies usually in- volve track circuit positioning, axle counter positioning, and intersec- tion inductive loop positioning. However, these methods are often limited by insufficient positioning accuracy and high maintenance costs. The application of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) in railway train control systems aims to reduce as much as possible the use of track-side infrastructures for train location awareness, including track circuits and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. Early in the 1990s, the United States introduces the concept of the

content.abstract_or_snippet: - fectively simulating realistic amplitude fluctuations observed in Radio Frequency (RF) interference sources. A similar signal interference set- ting is illustrated in Ref. [23]. Dynamic amplitude variations can sig- nificantly degrade GNSS signal reception, particularly in high-noise or contested environments. The transition FM Interference simulates frequency-modulated signal interference by generating two sinusoidal components with symmetrical frequency deviations around a carrier frequency centered at 1575.42 MHz, corresponding to the GNSS L1 band. These deviations are sampled from a Gaussian distribution with a standard deviation of 75 kHz, emulating the spectral characteristics of typical FM broadcast signals [24]. This approach captures the dynamic spectral spreading commonly observed in real-world RF interference. The transition Pulse Interference generates periodic pulse signals to si- mulate sudden and intense bursts of RF energy. It samples two random parameters: the pulse width Tp which represents the duration of each pulse, and the pulse interval Ti which defines the spacing between successive pulses. A uniformly distributed amplitude between 1 and 5 simulates the varying severity of the interference. At each time step, the model checks whether the current time modulo the total pulse period Tp + Ti falls within the pulse’s active duration. If so, an interference signal of the specified amplitude is emitted; otherwise, it remains zero. This periodic pulsed structure reflects typical burst-like interference patterns observed in GNSS environments [25], characterized by sharp, short-lived increases in signal strength followed by rapid attenuation. The input data enter the module through the input places Open Area and GNSS Signal, representing the GNSS satellites and the initial en- vironmental conditions. The place Bool Control and transition Mutex manage the logic for introducing signal interference, with the code in Mutex ensuring that the generation processes for different interference types do not conflict. Each interference signal is integrated into the GNSS data by firing the transition Interference. The GNSS data affected by signal interferences proceed to the observation output stage con- trolled by the place GNSS Number. The final results are outputted from the output place GNSS Observation. 3.2.2. Submodule Mountain Fig. 6 illustrates the shielding effect of mountainous terrain on GNSS signals. The main problem and challenge for the test can be illustrated as follows. 1) Train movement simulation: the train movement needs to be si- mulated as identical to the train operation, since when the train operates the GNSS receiver mounted on the train should receive the GNSS signal, and the train moving along the track needs to have the right occupation which needs to be demonstrated on the railway simulation platform. Thus, the train maneuver dynamics should be modeled in accordance with the correct train type. An accurate train dynamics model is fundamental to train movement algorithms. 3.1. GNSS testing standard and regulations As illustrated in the introduction part, the public service performance evaluation of GNSS covers two key aspects: SIS performance and system service performance. As the BeiDou Navigation Satellite System Open Service Performance Standard (BDS-OS-PS-3.0) defines, the BDS service performance should cover 5 core indicators: positioning accuracy, velocity measurement accuracy, timing accuracy, PDOP availability, and positioning service availability, as shown in Fig. 4. In terms of positioning accuracy, the specification clarifies the statistical standard of geographical location mea- surement error and specifies the allowable error range under different geographical areas and environmental conditions. The evaluation of speed measurement accuracy is concerned with the accuracy of the user equip- ment to measure the motion speed, which is used in dynamic scenarios, such as transportation and railway applications. Timing accuracy is aimed at high-precision time synchronization services, which are critical cap- abilities in fields such as communications, energy, and scientific experi- ments. The availability of PDOP is an important index to evaluate the effect of geometric configuration on positioning accuracy and to measure the degree of support of satellite layout to the stability of positioning solution. Location service availability measures the ability to provide continuous and reliable service at a given time and area from the perspective of the overall functionality of the system. In addition, the standard also specifies the measurement methods of this performance, including the evaluation environment, data collection method, calculation method, and evaluation cycle specific requirements. content.abstract_or_snippet: will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. GNSS/INS/Odometer sensor data fusion The ODO (Odometer) is the most commonly used train positioning method in existing train control systems. Wheel slide and slip during the train operation, as well as wheel diameter wear, may result in errors in the train speed measurement, and lead to error accumulation in the along-track location calculation results. Such errors will be brought into the INS/ODO integrated positioning system. The train positioning error by INS/ODO drifts over time until the position calibration results are available. Different from the Balises, results of GNSS positioning can provide the correction without relying on the track-side. Performance of a GNSS/INS/ODO integrated positioning system will be determined by the reliability and accuracy of GNSS. When the GNSS performance decreases or the GNSS signal is blocked for a long time, the result of INS will not be guaranteed effectively [6]. Therefore, the availability and observation quality of GNSS greatly affect the precision and effective- ness of train positioning using INS and ODO. The key issue in realizing the multi-sensor integrated train positioning is the sensor fusion logic. It is in charge of combining the measurements from multiple sensors, improving the reliability and fault tolerance of the integrated system, providing suitable auxiliary information for the ex- isting location determination system, and simplifying the system com- plexity over those track-side-relied solutions. Among different sensor fu- sion algorithms, Federated Kalman Filter (FKF) is a distributed cascaded integrated sensor fusion method and widely considered in applications because of its flexibility, low computational effort, and excellent fault tolerance capability. FKF consists of several local filters and a main filter, in which the system information is allocated in the local filters and then fused in the main filter. For the GNSS/INS/ODO integrated train posi- tioning system based on the FKF, INS is integrated due to its advantages in high output rate, autonomy and reliability. Considering the INS-related local filtering structures, two local filters for GNSS/INS and INS/ODO can 48 Fig. 1. Architecture of GNSS/INS/ODO sensor data fusion for integrated train positioning. be established, as shown in Fig. 1. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con- ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor- mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re- latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work. GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi- tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys- tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa- tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ- ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the anti- jamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-j

content.abstract_or_snippet: and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. Early in the 1990s, the United States introduces the concept of the Positive Train control System (PTS) [1], which relies on Global Posi- tioning System (GPS), accelerometers, and fiber-optic gyroscopes for the train positioning function. By December 2020, the United States achieved 100 % implementation of Positive Train Control (PTC) across all required railroads, marking a historic milestone in rail safety and interoperability [2]. CLUG 2.0 project [3] launched in 2023 in Europe aims to enhance the European Rail Traffic Management System (ERTMS) by integrating an on-board GNSS+EGNOS-based multi-sensor fusion architecture. In China, the China State Railway Group Co., Ltd. introduced a new train control system, the Chinese Train Control System-New (CTCS-N) [4], which proposed a “train-borne centric” train operation control method. This system incorporates on-board GNSS positioning, speed sensors, and electronic railway maps to achieve train localization. GNSS has been acknowledged as a pivotal enabler in im- proving the cost efficiency and automation level of the train control system. In complex railway operation scenarios, such as urban canyons, tunnels, and stations with cover, the limited GNSS signal visibility, and weak observation geometry would greatly decrease the positioning performance. Thus, the integration solutions are typically proposed for the train safety positioning. A typical integration solution for GNSS- based train positioning is based on the data fusion with both GNSS and Inertial Navigation System (INS) sensors. The GNSS/INS integrated solution is capable of providing full navigation solutions of position, velocity, and attitude in high positioning accuracy, high updating rate even under the GNSS limited scenario when the visible satellites are less than four. Additionally, there is an increasing concern that GNSS is also vulnerable to radio frequency jamming, due to the weak signal power of the GNSS signal. More and more GNSS disturbance events are reported because of jamming, in worldwide, including maritime applications [5,6], aviation applications [7], and also railway applications [8,9]. GNSS in-band interferences, for example, intentional jamming shows a source of potential threats to the safe, secure, and resilient operation of a dense railway system utilizing GNSS as the main positioning method

content.abstract_or_snippet: positioning system should be sufficiently robust and accurate to cope with various complex environments and severe weather conditions. Generally, in autonomous driving systems, it is a common practice to increase the overall positioning and mapping performance by fusing two or more sensors. Reference [152] proposed a camera–radar sensor fusion framework based on vehicle component (rear corner) detection and positioning to improve the stability of vehicle positioning. The main idea of this method is to enhance the azimuth accuracy of radar information by detecting and locating the rear corner of the target vehicle from the image. The proposed method can effectively track the corner points of the vehicle using only one visible corner even in occluded scenes. GPS may experience signal attenuation in indoor spaces and urban canyons, and IMU may drift over time due to error accumulation when integrating acceleration to determine velocity and position. Reference [153] uses an unscented Kalman filter (UKF) Bayesian filter to fuse GPS and IMU data. Reference [154] uses the error state Kalman filter (ESKF) and the piecewise Rauch–Tung–Striebel (RTS) smoothing algorithm to fuse GNSS and IMU to improve the positioning accuracy and robustness of GNSS and IMU sensors. The proposed ESKF-RTS method provides more accurate and reliable positioning for autonomous vehicles, especially in environments where GNSS signals are unreliable. Reference [155] proposes a fusion algorithm that combines the Kalman filter (KF) with a new INS/GPS neural network framework (GI-NN) to assist INS in reducing the accumulated navigation error during GPS signal loss. Reference [156] studies the information fusion of GPS and INS sensors using the Kalman filter. In order to improve the performance of the Kalman filter, a robust method using the Mahalanobis distance is adopted. In order to World Electr. Veh. J. 2025, 16, 20 25 of 34 compensate for the problem of GPS data interruption, an artificial neural network is used to assist GPS\INS information fusion. Visual sensors are important elements in autonomous driving positioning and mapping systems. Reference [157] proposes an improved multi- sensor fusion positioning system based on GNSS/LiDAR/Vision/IMU. With semi-tight coupling and graph optimization. The system tightly couples the raw observation data of LiDAR, vision and IMU, while adding the positioning information of GNSS as a loosely coupled component to reduce the cumulative error. The main contributions of this paper are as follows. 1) A novel CPN-based modeling framework for GNSS-based train po- sitioning systems is developed. 2) Dynamic influences of signal interferences (i.e., by AM signals, FM signals, pulse signals) and environmental obstructions (i.e., by mountains and tunnels) are incorporated into the model. 3) The performance of the GNSS-based train positioning system is quantitatively evaluated under various scenarios with the CPN model of the system. 2. Related works Over the past decades, GNSS-based systems have been extensively ap- plied to train positioning due to their global coverage and high accuracy under favorable conditions [1,7]. Despite these advantages, challenges such as signal interferences and terrain-induced occlusions continue to pose questions on high positioning accuracy and stability. Numerous studies have explored techniques to mitigate the impacts of signal interferences on GNSS- based positioning accuracy [8–12]. For example, Ref. [9] investigates the impact of continuous wave interference on GNSS receiver tracking perfor- mance, examining the extent of its influence by varying data bit durations or integration times. To address interference-related challenges, an inter- ference energy detection method and a comprehensive orientation tech- nology tailored for GNSS are introduced in Ref. [11]. Besides, a number of researches have examined the impact of environmental factors along railway lines on GNSS signal integrity. For instance, Ref. [13] explores methods of scenario-based weighted measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. Some studies have achieved high positioning accuracy in partially obstructed or challenging environ- ments by fusing multi-sensor data [6,

High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Fig. 8. The positioning errors and the velocity errors for the normal tracking solution and the INS-aided solution in scenario II. 5. Conclusion In this paper, a GNSS jamming protection solution is presented based on the INS-aided deep integration solution. The solution enables a self-contained PVT reference that is immune to RF signals. INS in- formation is sent to the receiver’s baseband, realizing the assistance to the receiver at the signal processing level. The measured velocity in- formation from INS is mapped to the LOS between the satellite and the vehicle to obtain the Doppler of relative motion. The Doppler is fed back to the NCO in the tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The the proposed solution demonstrates significant evaluation of improvements in positioning and velocity, enabling resilient train po- sitioning for securing next generation GNSS-based train control sys- tems. Future work will focus on refining mitigation techniques with extended studies for multi-constellation scenarios (e.g., BeiDou and Galileo), and exploring field tests in real lines to ensure the resilience of GNSS-based train localization systems in railway applications. High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 and the Doppler radar, also show great potentials in realizing the au- tonomous train positioning with a low cost over the Balise-based solu- tions [4]. However, it can be found that the single-sensor-based solution cannot satisfy the requirement of the train control system due to specific limitations and disadvantages in practical scenarios [5]. How to explore the capabilities of multiple perception sensors and break through the application-demand-oriented information fusion processing technology is the key to realize the satisfaction of novel train control systems with the train-centric design. Hence, it is important to overcome the key technologies of autonomous train positioning based on the fusion of GNSS and assistant sensors. By developing a novel train control system with dynamic configuration of the trains’ interval, reduced involvement of track-side equipments, and enhanced maintainability of the on-board train positioning system, the advantages of the train-centric mode will be practically reflected and utilized for specific railway lines. In this paper, key technologies of the novel train control system with GNSS- based autonomous perception are investigated and demonstrated in the practical environment, which illustrate the necessity and advantages of the utilization of GNSS, especially BDS (BeiDou Navigation Satellite System), in future safety-related railway systems in China. 2. Multi-sensor data fusion In existing railway signaling systems, the track occupancy of the train is determined by the track circuit, and the exact along-track lo- cation of the train is calculated by integrating both the train-borne odometer (or Doppler radar) and track-side Balises. The novel train- centric signaling system determines the track occupancy and train lo- cation without relying on those track-side facilities. Thus, raw mea- surements from multiple train-borne sensors will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. Heterogeneous sensor data fusion can eliminate information redundancy and loss, and provide reliable, stable, and efficient environmental perception capabilities, but the cost of the system will increase accordingly. 2.4. Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)/Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) The Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) and Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) are key components in autonomous driving systems, helping to determine the position, speed, and orientation of the vehicle. GNSS is a satellite-based navigation system that provides precise positioning and timing information anywhere on Earth. By triangulat- ing signals from multiple satellites, a GNSS receiver can calculate the vehicle’s position (latitude, longitude, and altitude) and speed. GNSS is widely used for global positioning and mapping, especially in open areas such as highways. In autonomous driving, it is one of the most commonly used vehicle positioning sensors. GNSS provides precise global positioning, which enables accurate positioning in open environments such as highways or rural roads. Differential GPS (DGPS), Real-Time Kinematic GPS (RTK-GPS), and Precise Point Positioning (PPP) technologies can be used to improve the accuracy of GNSS. The differential global positioning system (DGPS) operation consists of a reference station and a rover. Both stations use GPS receivers to receive positioning data from GPS satellites, use the positioning data collected by the reference station to calculate the positioning error, and transmit the error correction to the rover to improve the positioning accuracy [27]. RTK can achieve real-time centimeter-level positioning accuracy through double-difference ambigu- ity resolution (AR) [28]. Compared with differential techniques, precise point positioning (PPP) has a significant advantage in that it can accurately determine the position of a GNSS rover receiver by using external corrections from the Internet or dedicated correction satellites [29]. While both DGPS and RTK can achieve high positioning accuracy, GPS signal interruptions in urban environments and tunnels remain a challenge for these sensors. The IMU can provide information about the vehicle’s attitude, velocity, and direction. The IMU helps determine vehicle motion between GNSS updates and ensures

Thus, the safe train can be calculated by combining the HPL and error range of two the envelope confidence interval as (5) 5. State perception performance evaluation 5.1. RAMS architecture for railway signaling The performance indices of GNSS-based positioning are different from the requirements of railway systems, especially for the safety-related railway applications. With the EN50126 standard definition, the RAMS (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability & Safety) architecture is presented with different requirements to specific railway systems and functions. 1) Reliability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform a specified function under specified conditions and within a specified time period. The evaluation indicators with respect to the reliability include failure rate, MTBF (Mean Time Between Failure) and MUT (Mean Up Time). 2) Availability. It refers to the extent to which a product or a system is in a workable or usable state at any moment when the required external re- sources are met or when it starts to perform its tasks. The availability of a product is a composite representation of its reliability and maintainability. 3) Maintainability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform specified maintenance work under the specified conditions and within the specified time using the specified procedures and resources for maintenance. 4) Safety. It refers to the ability to not incur risks that may cause damage. The safety requirements of the GNSS autonomous positioning- based train control system include functional safety requirements and other safety requirements. Among them, functional safety requirements are described by SIL (Safety Integrity Level), and different SIL levels are defined by the THR (Tolerable Hazard Rate). For a novel train control system based on autonomous perception using GNSS, the RAMS indices also have to be considered in the design and operation phases. The relationship of RAMS indices in the railway domain is shown in Fig. 7. If the reliability and maintainability are met, and long-term maintenance and control are performed according to the environment in which the system is located, the safety and availability of the system can also be met. 51 =zxgkkf[(),()](

Safety stands for the ability to eliminate the risk of un- acceptable damage to a positioning unit. The railway sector uses safety integrity level to describe the likelihood that a system achieves the required safety function within a specified time under all specified conditions. The SIL specifies the safety integrity requirement for the safety functions assigned to the safety-related systems. Different SIL levels can be described in Table 5. The risk assessment needs to be performed for the safe design of train positioning architecture for the railway signaling applications. The risk assessment results can be classified as risky and the risk-free cases. Therefore, the output of safety assessment can be defined as the frequency of risky event Prisk. It has to be noted that the above-mentioned indices considering both the RAMS and GNSS systems are closely correlated, which means that performance evaluation is carried out based on a systemic measure. The relationship among the performance indicators for GNSS-based train positioning and state perception can be described as Fig. 8. 6. Field demonstration and analysis Based on the development of the novel train control system, an advanced train positioning unit for the railway train control applica- tion was designed and developed. The unit is realized based on the safe platform with a 2-out-of-2 (2oo2) redundant architecture. Under the safe platform architecture, the sensor data collection boards and communication boards are designed independently. Two sensor data collection boards are involved to independently collect raw measure- ments from the GPS/BDS receivers, INS sensors and the odometer. The communication boards are designed for information interaction be- tween the train positioning unit and the ATP kernel. The core position calculation unit realizes the sensor data fusion and location determi- nation using the presented technologies in this paper. In 2021, a field test was carried out at the Haergai-Muli railway. The test section is more than 120 kilometers with a high altitude between 3200 and 4200 m. Five stations are involved in the field test section, including Huancang, Chaidaer II, Chaidaer I, Wailihada and Jiangcang. Trackmap database of the test section was measured and generated before the field demonstration. Specific track-side GPS/BDS differ- ential stations were developed and installed to enhance the perfor- mance of train-borne receivers. Field data collection for trackmap generation and the equipment

Relationship of different indices in the RAMS architecture. 5.2. Indices of integrated train state perception It can be found that the GNSS-enabled train state perception can be evaluated using different indices from the railway-specified RAMS ar- chitecture. The relationship between GNSS-related performance index system and the RAMS definitions has to be investigated to achieve ef- fective evaluation of the GNSS-based applications in the railway do- main. The performance indices corresponding to GNSS are summarized as follows. 1) Accuracy. As the most essential evaluation indicator for the GNSS-enabled train positioning, the concept of accuracy mainly in- cludes static accuracy and dynamic accuracy. Accuracy can be divided into two parts, including correctness and precision. As described in Table 2, it can be expressed by the position error and the degree of confidence, respectively. 2) Integrity. Through the integration of static trackmap information, sensor data fusion for BDS/GPS re- ceivers, INS sensors and the odometer measurements were real- timely achieved with a high availability level under the complex and time-changing operation environment. Both the static and dynamic test cases were involved in the field demonstration. Table 6 sum- marizes the accuracy results of train positioning under different system modes. The results from field demonstration illustrate that involvement of the presented key technologies, including the safe platform and multi- source sensor fusion mechanism, effectively guarantees the perfor- mance of GNSS-based autonomous train positioning and state percep- tion even under complex and time-varying operational conditions. The utilization of the differential GNSS technology and the integrity mon- itoring logic makes it possible of achieving the high accuracy and ro- bustness against the possible difficult or even critical conditions. The field test results demonstrate the feasibility and the advantages of utilizing BDS in realizing a novel autonomous train control system with a train-centric design, which is of great significance in the future high- speed and plateau railway applications with the enhanced environ- mental flexibility and whole life cycle cost efficiency. 7. Conclusion The application of GNSS technology is one of the representative characteristics of intelligent railway systems in the future. However, there are specific requirements to the GNSS-based train positioning and state perception in the railway signaling applications due to the critical re- quirement to the safety. Thus, the utilization of GNSS has to be enhanced by the specific technical solutions. The sensor data fusion technique is of great significance to enhance the service availability under degraded or even a critical GNSS observing condition. To ensure the compatibility to the existing system specifications, the VB technology enables the in- tegration of the GNSS capabilities into the train control system and meanwhile the train integrity monitoring logic allows the opportunity to realize moving block-based train operation control through the dynamic identification of the track occupancy and safe envelope of the train. By investigating the relationship between the GNSS performance system and the RAMS specification, the performance evaluation of the GNSS-based train positioning can be effectively realized. It can be found that the in- volvement of these key technologies consolidates the autonomy and cap- abilities of the train-borne system, which releases the requirement to the track-side facilities like track circuit and Balises. Principle of length-based train integrity monitoring based on GNSS and trackmap. Due to the existence of positioning error, especially the uncertainty of EOT positioning resulted by the constrained satellite visibility, the uncertainty of the derived train length has to be concerned [12]. As shown in Fig. 6, an uncertainty buffer has to be added to the estimated HOT and EOT position to form an enhanced “envelope” for the train integrity determination purpose. A conservative evaluation of the un- certainty ranges is necessary due to the safety assurance target, and thus several factors related to the positioning performance have to be investigated, including the measurement noise, state estimation error and the potential fault(s) and failure(s). With this consideration, the derived train length would be larger than truth, and thus the threshold for train integrity identification has to be effectively determined to avoid false alarm to the abnormal train integrity events. The uncertainties in HOT and EOT positioning results lead to the es- timation deviation of the train length. When the quality of GNSS posi- tioning degrades with the change of the observation condition, the de- viation of train length estimation would be large and thus the failure of accurate train integrity monitoring may occur. Therefore, the consistency evaluation of the HOT/EOT relative speed can be involved to consolidate the integrity status identification through multiple information resources. Considering the errors in position and speed measurements of the both ends, it would be necessary to constrain the errors through the filtering operation. It can be found that there are mainly two specific trends to the monitoring variables, including relative static and relative acceleration according to the HOT-EOT-based train length and relative speed estimation. Therefore, a sliding window strategy can be involved for mean filtering, and then the filtered data in the window can be fitted by LS-based linear fitting. According to the fitting results, the qualita- tive trend of the data in the sliding window can be recognized to ana- lyze the changing status of the HOT/EOT relative speed and the esti- mation deviation of the train length. 4.2. content.abstract_or_snippet: used to build UMl diagrams was the Rational Rose Modeler Edition [9]. While building the model we exploited all the syntactic controls and automations of the Rational Rose Modeler application, which constituted a first congruity check on the model under construction. Some examples: as the model grows up, the already defined links are automatically added by the application; in sequence diagrams, it is not possible to call an operation which has not been already defined in class diagrams; the accessibility of attributes and operations (public, protected, private) is automatically checked; etc. Next section will provide a general introduction to ERTMS/ETcS. 4.1 ERTMS/ETcS level 2 system implementation ERTMS/ETcS is the specification of a standard aiming at improving safety, performance and interoperability of European railways [7]. In Italy, the so-called level 2 specification of ERTMS/ETcS is implemented in high-speed railway lines. ERTMS/ETcS specifies three main subsystems: trackside, on-board and lineside. The trackside subsystem is the ground part of the overall system, and manages railway signaling to ensure safe train separation and rout- ing. The most important trackside subsystem is the Radio Block center, which has the aim of collecting track and train information in order to provide trains with necessary data: Move- ment Authorities (MA), that is the distance trains are authorized to move on; Static Speed Profiles (SSP), that is maximum speed limits allowed by the track; possible Emergency Stops (ES). The on-board control system performs train protection by controlling train speed against the elaborated dynamic speed profiles (or braking curves). The lineside is constituted by the so-called balises, which are devices positioned between the track lines, which have the aim to transmit geographical positioning data to the trains passing over them; such data is used by the on-board system in order to build and send position reports to the trackside. The Radio Block center is responsible of providing train headways by using data received in train position reports and delivering the correct MA, SSP and ES messages to the trains. In order to detect the necessary route and Track circuit (Tc) occupation status, RBc is con- nected to the national Interlocking (IXl) system, which is not standardized in ERTMS. Moreover, the RBc is usually able to manage a limited number of track sections, thus it must be connected to other adjacent RBcs in order to allow for the

content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train- centric railway signaling Baigen Caia,b,⁎ a School of Computer and Information Technology, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c School of Geodesy and Geomatics, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China d GNSS Research Center, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China e Space Engineering University, Beijing 101416, China , Jingnan Liuc,d, Xurong Donge, Jiang Liub ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: Railway signaling Train control Global Navigation Satellite System Sensor data fusion Virtual Balise Train integrity Performance evaluation The application of Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) in the intelligent railway systems is rapidly developing all over the world. With the GNSS-based train positioning and moving state perception, the au- tonomy and flexibility of a novel train control system can be greatly enhanced over the existing solutions relying on the track-side facilities. Considering the safety critical features of the railway signaling applications, the GNSS stand-alone mode may not be sufficient to satisfy the practical requirements. In this paper, the key technologies for applying GNSS in novel train-centric railway signaling systems are investigated, including the multi-sensor data fusion, Virtual Balise (VB) capturing and messaging, train integrity monitoring and system performance evaluation. According to the practical characteristics of the novel train control system under the moving block mode, the details of the key technologies are introduced. Field demonstration results of a novel train control system using the presented technologies under the practical railway operation conditions are presented to il- lustrate the achievable performance feature of autonomous train state perception using BeiDou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) and related solutions. It reveals the great potentials of these key technologies in the next generation train control system and other GNSS-based railway implementations. 1. Introduction The train control system plays a significant role in ensuring the safety of railway operation and improving the transportation efficiency. Chinese Train Control Systems (CTCSs), including CTCS level 2 and level 3, have been

content.abstract_or_snippet: . Safe train envelope estimation For GNSS-based train positioning and train integrity monitoring, the uncertainty in state estimation has to be concerned to ensure the safety and efficiency of train operation by utilizing the integrity monitoring result. Under the moving block train control scheme, where the occu- pancy of the track segments is determined by the practical length of the train, it is necessary to add a reasonable margin to the raw HOT and EOT location to form a safe occupancy range [13]. The safe train position has to cover the most unfavorable conditions. With this consideration, the margin should be sufficiently large to realize a high possibility to protect the unsatisfied situations. However, an excessive margin will lead to unreasonable identification of track occupancy, which may greatly af- fect the operational efficiency. To realize the expected balance level between the safety and efficiency characteristics, a constrained safe margin has to be adopted on the basis of safety and rationality, so as to reduce the train tracing interval and improve the system efficiency. The essence of the safe train envelope is to realize the safety as- surance of train control by describing the reliability of train positioning. The safe train envelope extends the positioning output based on the evaluation of the uncertainty of positioning errors, and provides a safe overlay to the most possible position derived by the HOT/EOT positioning unit. The safe margin consists of the HPL by integrity monitoring result and the confidential interval of the positioning error. The HPL, which indicates the radius of a circle in the horizontal plane with its center being at the true position to describe the region assured to contain the indicated horizontal position, is usually regarded as the safety boundary of positioning result by GNSS. However, the existence of the integrity risk may lead to an unbounded position error that exceeds the HPL. Without the high-level referencing system during the dynamic operation, it is difficult to accurately evaluate the relia- bility of the safety boundary by HPL. In order to guarantee the cred- ibility of the safe margin for envelope evaluation, it is necessary to estimate the possible range of the positioning error by a specific error confidence interval strategy. Considering the track-constrained along- track operation of railway trains, the error confidence interval indicates a one-dimensional along-track interval that can be represented by the uncertainties in state prediction, measurement update and the trackmap-based location determination, which means (4) and where confidence interval at instant k, result, geographic information from the trackmap database. represent the lower and upper limits of the indicates the user measurement represents the prior denotes the prediction result, and

content.abstract_or_snippet: 4. Train integrity monitoring The connection mode between the train carriages is a rigid connection. When the train is grouped, the length of the train will be fixed covering the length of all the locomotives and carriages (or EMUs for the high-speed railway) [10,11]. In the practical operation, a normal train integrity state indicates that the relative speed of the HOT (Head-of-Train) and EOT (End-of-Train) is zero and the projected HOT-EOT interval equals to the nominal train length. When decoupling occurs, the train will be in an incomplete state, the thus the EOT will lose the traction force and de- celerate until the speed falls to zero. An abnormal integrity status will result in an increased HOT-EOT relative speed, leading to an increasing HOT-EOT distance. Therefore, the train integrity state can be identified by evaluating the HOT/EOT relevant state and the train length. 4.1. Train integrity monitoring based on GNSS and trackmap Through the monitoring of along-track position, speed and direction of the HOT and EOT with the aid of the trackmap database, the train integrity status can be determined through the dynamic evaluation of the train length. Based on the POI (Point-of-Interest) set from the trackmap, where represent latitude, longitude, altitude and mileage of the POI, map matching can be carried out to determine the along-track location (1D mileage that is not affected by the curve shape of the railway line) of HOT and EOT for train length ( ) evaluation, which means Fig. 4. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. Some studies have achieved high positioning accuracy in partially obstructed or challenging environ- ments by fusing multi-sensor data [6,14,15]. In contrast to studies that mainly investigate GNSS signal inter- ferences in isolation, this paper integrates environmental factors and signal interferences into the formal model of the positioning system. By utilizing model simulation, the proposed approach enables a quantita- tive evaluation of the system’s performance. This method provides a systematic analysis and verification of the interactions among signal interferences, environmental conditions, and train positioning. Due to the rigorous mathematical foundation, formal methods have gained recognition as effective tools for system modeling and verification. In this work, we adopt CPNs [16] as the formal modeling framework to study the impact of signal interferences and environ- mental factors on GNSS-based train positioning. Petri nets are formal modeling languages that are well-known for systems characterized as concurrent, synchronous, distributed, etc. Some application examples adopting petri nets as the means of description for railway signaling systems can be found in Refs. [17–22]. 3. System modeling with CPNs Fig. 1 illustrates the modeling framework of a GNSS-based train positioning system comprising the following main parts: GNSS Re- ceiver, Environment Scenarios, Interference Signals, GNSS Position Solution, and Evaluation Module. The module GNSS Receiver selects specific environment scenarios and receives GNSS signals. The module Environment Scenarios is composed of three submodules: Open Area Scenario, Mountain Occlusion Scenario, and Tunnel and Exit Tunnel Scenario. These submodules simulate various real-world conditions while generating GNSS signal interferences, i.e., GNSS signals are interfered with by AM, FM, and pulse signals. In the module GNSS Positioning Solution, the EKF algorithm is employed to process interfered GNSS signals for estimating the train’s position. The module Evaluation compares the calculated position with the reference position and outputs the positioning error. Fig. 2 clearly illustrates the hierarchical relationships between modules of the CPN model, providing a structured view of their layered organization. The declarations of the model are categorized and summarized in two tables for clarity. Table 1 provides the colset declarations and their respective descriptions, while Table 2 provides the variable declara- tions and their respective descriptions. 3.1. Top-level of the positioning system model The top-level of the CPN model is
