content.abstract_or_snippet: . Safe train envelope estimation For GNSS-based train positioning and train integrity monitoring, the uncertainty in state estimation has to be concerned to ensure the safety and efficiency of train operation by utilizing the integrity monitoring result. Under the moving block train control scheme, where the occu- pancy of the track segments is determined by the practical length of the train, it is necessary to add a reasonable margin to the raw HOT and EOT location to form a safe occupancy range [13]. The safe train position has to cover the most unfavorable conditions. With this consideration, the margin should be sufficiently large to realize a high possibility to protect the unsatisfied situations. However, an excessive margin will lead to unreasonable identification of track occupancy, which may greatly af- fect the operational efficiency. To realize the expected balance level between the safety and efficiency characteristics, a constrained safe margin has to be adopted on the basis of safety and rationality, so as to reduce the train tracing interval and improve the system efficiency. The essence of the safe train envelope is to realize the safety as- surance of train control by describing the reliability of train positioning. The safe train envelope extends the positioning output based on the evaluation of the uncertainty of positioning errors, and provides a safe overlay to the most possible position derived by the HOT/EOT positioning unit. The safe margin consists of the HPL by integrity monitoring result and the confidential interval of the positioning error. The HPL, which indicates the radius of a circle in the horizontal plane with its center being at the true position to describe the region assured to contain the indicated horizontal position, is usually regarded as the safety boundary of positioning result by GNSS. However, the existence of the integrity risk may lead to an unbounded position error that exceeds the HPL. Without the high-level referencing system during the dynamic operation, it is difficult to accurately evaluate the relia- bility of the safety boundary by HPL. In order to guarantee the cred- ibility of the safe margin for envelope evaluation, it is necessary to estimate the possible range of the positioning error by a specific error confidence interval strategy. Considering the track-constrained along- track operation of railway trains, the error confidence interval indicates a one-dimensional along-track interval that can be represented by the uncertainties in state prediction, measurement update and the trackmap-based location determination, which means (4) and where confidence interval at instant k, result, geographic information from the trackmap database. represent the lower and upper limits of the indicates the user measurement represents the prior denotes the prediction result, and

Through the integration of static trackmap information, sensor data fusion for BDS/GPS re- ceivers, INS sensors and the odometer measurements were real- timely achieved with a high availability level under the complex and time-changing operation environment. Both the static and dynamic test cases were involved in the field demonstration. Table 6 sum- marizes the accuracy results of train positioning under different system modes. The results from field demonstration illustrate that involvement of the presented key technologies, including the safe platform and multi- source sensor fusion mechanism, effectively guarantees the perfor- mance of GNSS-based autonomous train positioning and state percep- tion even under complex and time-varying operational conditions. The utilization of the differential GNSS technology and the integrity mon- itoring logic makes it possible of achieving the high accuracy and ro- bustness against the possible difficult or even critical conditions. The field test results demonstrate the feasibility and the advantages of utilizing BDS in realizing a novel autonomous train control system with a train-centric design, which is of great significance in the future high- speed and plateau railway applications with the enhanced environ- mental flexibility and whole life cycle cost efficiency. 7. Conclusion The application of GNSS technology is one of the representative characteristics of intelligent railway systems in the future. However, there are specific requirements to the GNSS-based train positioning and state perception in the railway signaling applications due to the critical re- quirement to the safety. Thus, the utilization of GNSS has to be enhanced by the specific technical solutions. The sensor data fusion technique is of great significance to enhance the service availability under degraded or even a critical GNSS observing condition. To ensure the compatibility to the existing system specifications, the VB technology enables the in- tegration of the GNSS capabilities into the train control system and meanwhile the train integrity monitoring logic allows the opportunity to realize moving block-based train operation control through the dynamic identification of the track occupancy and safe envelope of the train. By investigating the relationship between the GNSS performance system and the RAMS specification, the performance evaluation of the GNSS-based train positioning can be effectively realized. It can be found that the in- volvement of these key technologies consolidates the autonomy and cap- abilities of the train-borne system, which releases the requirement to the track-side facilities like track circuit and Balises. content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train- centric railway signaling Baigen Caia,b,⁎ a School of Computer and Information Technology, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c School of Geodesy and Geomatics, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China d GNSS Research Center, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China e Space Engineering University, Beijing 101416, China , Jingnan Liuc,d, Xurong Donge, Jiang Liub ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: Railway signaling Train control Global Navigation Satellite System Sensor data fusion Virtual Balise Train integrity Performance evaluation The application of Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) in the intelligent railway systems is rapidly developing all over the world. With the GNSS-based train positioning and moving state perception, the au- tonomy and flexibility of a novel train control system can be greatly enhanced over the existing solutions relying on the track-side facilities. Considering the safety critical features of the railway signaling applications, the GNSS stand-alone mode may not be sufficient to satisfy the practical requirements. In this paper, the key technologies for applying GNSS in novel train-centric railway signaling systems are investigated, including the multi-sensor data fusion, Virtual Balise (VB) capturing and messaging, train integrity monitoring and system performance evaluation. According to the practical characteristics of the novel train control system under the moving block mode, the details of the key technologies are introduced. Field demonstration results of a novel train control system using the presented technologies under the practical railway operation conditions are presented to il- lustrate the achievable performance feature of autonomous train state perception using BeiDou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) and related solutions. It reveals the great potentials of these key technologies in the next generation train control system and other GNSS-based railway implementations. 1. Principle of length-based train integrity monitoring based on GNSS and trackmap. Due to the existence of positioning error, especially the uncertainty of EOT positioning resulted by the constrained satellite visibility, the uncertainty of the derived train length has to be concerned [12]. As shown in Fig. 6, an uncertainty buffer has to be added to the estimated HOT and EOT position to form an enhanced “envelope” for the train integrity determination purpose. A conservative evaluation of the un- certainty ranges is necessary due to the safety assurance target, and thus several factors related to the positioning performance have to be investigated, including the measurement noise, state estimation error and the potential fault(s) and failure(s). With this consideration, the derived train length would be larger than truth, and thus the threshold for train integrity identification has to be effectively determined to avoid false alarm to the abnormal train integrity events. The uncertainties in HOT and EOT positioning results lead to the es- timation deviation of the train length. When the quality of GNSS posi- tioning degrades with the change of the observation condition, the de- viation of train length estimation would be large and thus the failure of accurate train integrity monitoring may occur. Therefore, the consistency evaluation of the HOT/EOT relative speed can be involved to consolidate the integrity status identification through multiple information resources. Considering the errors in position and speed measurements of the both ends, it would be necessary to constrain the errors through the filtering operation. It can be found that there are mainly two specific trends to the monitoring variables, including relative static and relative acceleration according to the HOT-EOT-based train length and relative speed estimation. Therefore, a sliding window strategy can be involved for mean filtering, and then the filtered data in the window can be fitted by LS-based linear fitting. According to the fitting results, the qualita- tive trend of the data in the sliding window can be recognized to ana- lyze the changing status of the HOT/EOT relative speed and the esti- mation deviation of the train length. 4.2. updated_hypotheses.statement: In Tunnel- und Tunnel-Übergangsszenarien (Einfahrt/ Ausfahrt) ist GNSS-basierte Zugortung zeitweise nicht verfügbar bzw. zeigt deutlich erhöhte Positionsfehler, weil Signale im Tunnel vollständig abgeschattet werden und beim Wiedererfassen nach der Ausfahrt Multipath/Obstruktionen große Initialfehler verursachen.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Sehr gute semantische Passung zur Evidenz: Das CPN-Performancepapier beschreibt im Tunnelszenario komplette GNSS-Abschattung („Inside the Tunnel“: Verlust der Positionierungsfähigkeit) sowie große Initialfehler in der Reacquisition-Phase nach der Ausfahrt aufgrund von Multipath/Obstruktionen; mittlerer Fehler im Tunnel 5.667 m. Keine Gegenbelege in den bereitgestellten Dokumenten.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: In-band bzw. nahbandige RF-Interferenzen (AM/FM/Puls sowie Sweep/Chirp) können GNSS-Empfänger im Bahnumfeld so beeinträchtigen, dass RF-Front-End und Code-/Carrier-Tracking-Loops keinen stabilen Lock halten, was Positions- und Geschwindigkeitsfehler stark erhöht; INS-aided Deep Integration mit Doppler-Aiding und verengter Loop-Bandbreite reduziert diese Degradation messbar unter Jamming (z.B. ~63% Positionsfehler-Reduktion in semi-physischen Tests).
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Jamming-Studie erläutert, dass Jamming RF-Front-End sowie Carrier/Code-Tracking-Loops stört und Lock-Verlust bzw. Performance-Degradation verursacht; zudem werden experimentell deutliche Fehlerreduktionen durch INS-aided Deep Integration mit Doppler-Aiding und verengter Loop-Bandbreite berichtet (Positionsfehler −63.47%, Geschwindigkeitsfehler −58.47%). Das CPN-Papier modelliert zusätzlich AM/FM/Puls-Interferenzen als signifikante Accuracy-Degradation. Keine widersprechende Evidenz im Korpus.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Eine GNSS/INS/Odometer-Integration mit verteilter Filterarchitektur (Federated Kalman Filter mit GNSS/INS- und INS/ODO-Teilfiltern) erhöht Fault-Tolerance und Verfügbarkeit gegenüber GNSS-Degradationen; die erreichbare Leistung bleibt jedoch stark von GNSS-Verfügbarkeit/-Beobachtungsqualität abhängig, und bei langem GNSS-Ausfall driftet die INS/ODO-Lösung und ist nicht effektiv garantierbar.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Gute Passung: Das train-centric-signaling Dokument beschreibt Odometer-Fehlerquellen (Slip/Slide, Wear) und daraus resultierende Fehlerakkumulation; es betont, dass GNSS/INS/ODO-Leistung maßgeblich von GNSS-Zuverlässigkeit/Beobachtungsqualität abhängt und bei längerem GNSS-Blockade/Degradation INS-Ergebnis nicht effektiv garantiert ist. FKF wird als flexible, verteilte Sensorfusionslogik mit erhöhter Fault-Tolerance genannt. Keine Gegenbelege im bereitgestellten Material.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Für Moving-Block/Train-centric Signaling wird GNSS-basierte Ortung zulassungs-/sicherheitsrelevant erst dann nutzbar, wenn GNSS-Performancekennzahlen (Accuracy/Integrity/Availability) systematisch in RAMS-/SIL-nahe Größen überführt werden (z.B. Integrity-Überwachung mit HPL/HAL/TTA, Safe-Train-Envelope aus HPL und Fehlerkonfidenzintervall, und 2oo2-Plattform-Redundanz), um gefährliche (undetected) Fehlerzustände zu begrenzen.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Weitgehend gedeckt: Dokumente stellen RAMS nach EN50126 dar und heben die Notwendigkeit hervor, GNSS-Indizes (Accuracy/Integrity/Availability) auf railway-spezifische RAMS/Sicherheitsgrößen zu beziehen. Integrity-Parameter (HPL/HAL/TTA) werden explizit definiert; Safe-Train-Envelope wird als Kombination aus HPL und Fehler-Konfidenzintervall beschrieben. Feld-/Plattformbeschreibung nennt 2oo2-Redundanzarchitektur zur sicheren Umsetzung. Keine widersprechende Evidenz in den bereitgestellten Auszügen.

challenge_findings.description: Tunnel- und Übergangsphasen führen zu kompletter GNSS-Blockade im Tunnel und zu großen Reacquisition-Initialfehlern durch Multipath/Obstruktionen nach der Ausfahrt; dadurch zeitweise Nichtverfügbarkeit bzw. stark erhöhte Fehler.

In-/nahbandige RF-Interferenzen/Jamming stören RF-Front-End und Code/Carrier-Tracking-Loops bis hin zu Lock-Verlust; INS-aided Deep Integration (Doppler-Aiding, Loop-Bandbreitenverengung) reduziert Positions- und Geschwindigkeitsfehler deutlich (z.B. ~63%/58%).

Obwohl GNSS/INS/ODO-Fusion (z.B. FKF) Fault-Tolerance/Verfügbarkeit verbessern soll, bleibt die Gesamtleistung stark von GNSS-Verfügbarkeit und -Beobachtungsqualität abhängig; bei langem GNSS-Ausfall driftet INS/ODO (verstärkt durch Odometer-Slip/Wear) und ist nicht garantiert wirksam.

Für sicherheitsrelevante Nutzung (Moving Block) wird die Überführung GNSS-typischer Kennzahlen in RAMS/Safety-Logik adressiert: Integrity-Parameter (HPL/HAL/TTA) und Safe-Train-Envelope (HPL + Fehlerkonfidenzintervall) sowie sichere Plattformarchitektur (2oo2).

Forschungsfrage: Vor welchen Herausforderungen steht der Einsatz von GNSS in der digitalen Schieneninfrastruktur und wie trägt die Integration von Inertialsensoren (INS) dazu bei, einen ausfallsicheren Bahnbetrieb zu gewährleisten?

Erkannte Domänen: rail

research_plan.reasoning: Die Frage zielt auf den robusten Einsatz von GNSS für Positionierung/Ortung in der digitalen Schieneninfrastruktur (z.B. ETCS/ATO/Moving Block) und die Rolle von INS-Sensorfusion zur Erhöhung von Verfügbarkeit, Integrität und Resilienz (Tunnels, Abschattung, Multipath, Jamming/Spoofing). Dafür werden (1) Herausforderungen/Fehlermodi von GNSS im Bahnumfeld, (2) Integrations- und Fusionsansätze GNSS+INS inkl. Algorithmen und Leistungskennzahlen sowie (3) normative/zulassungsrelevante Anforderungen und (4) Referenzarchitekturen/Projektberichte in der Bahn benötigt.

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: terrain, with the elevation direction being particularly sensitive to these factors. Tunnel scenarios show the most pronounced degradation with a mean error of 5.6670 m. This dramatic increase results from severe signal blockages in tunnels and the instability during signal reacquisition after exiting the tunnel. Fig. 10 illustrates the evolution of position error in the tunnel en- vironment. During the “Inside the Tunnel” phase, GNSS signals are completely blocked by the surrounding structure, resulting in a loss of positioning capability (represented by the gray shaded region). Upon exiting the tunnel (marked by the vertical dashed line), the system begins to reacquire GNSS signals; however, due to severe multipath effects and signal obstructions, the initial position errors are sig- nificantly large. As the train moves further away from the tunnel exit, the surrounding environment improves, leading to a gradual reduction in error, which eventually stabilizes. This trend highlights the critical Fig. 10. Position errors in tunnel scenario. impact of tunnel environments and their transitions on GNSS-based positioning performance. 5. Conclusion This paper presents a CPNs-based approach to evaluate the perfor- mances of GNSS-based train positioning systems under real-world op- erational conditions. By constructing a hierarchical CPN model, the study successfully models the process of GNSS signal reception and interferences, as well as the impacts of complex environmental factors on the GNSS-based train positioning. The simulation results emphasize that signal interferences (i.e., interfered by AM, FM, and pulse signals) have a significant impact on the GNSS-based positioning accuracy, with FM interference exerting the most pronounced effects, particularly in the vertical direction. Environmental factors like terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding further degrade signal visibility and increase po- sitioning errors. The model also identifies critical challenges during signal reacquisition, particularly in tunnel transitions, which exacerbate error growth. While the proposed approach effectively demonstrates the impact of various signal interferences and environmental conditions on the per- formance of the GNSS-based train positioning, future research could explore the integration of additional signal interference mitigation techniques to improve the accuracy and stability of the GNSS-based positioning for railway applications. CRediT authorship contribution statement Siqi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Data curation. Shuting Chen: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Daohua Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jiang Liu: Writing – review

content.abstract_or_snippet: will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. GNSS/INS/Odometer sensor data fusion The ODO (Odometer) is the most commonly used train positioning method in existing train control systems. Wheel slide and slip during the train operation, as well as wheel diameter wear, may result in errors in the train speed measurement, and lead to error accumulation in the along-track location calculation results. Such errors will be brought into the INS/ODO integrated positioning system. The train positioning error by INS/ODO drifts over time until the position calibration results are available. Different from the Balises, results of GNSS positioning can provide the correction without relying on the track-side. Performance of a GNSS/INS/ODO integrated positioning system will be determined by the reliability and accuracy of GNSS. When the GNSS performance decreases or the GNSS signal is blocked for a long time, the result of INS will not be guaranteed effectively [6]. Therefore, the availability and observation quality of GNSS greatly affect the precision and effective- ness of train positioning using INS and ODO. The key issue in realizing the multi-sensor integrated train positioning is the sensor fusion logic. It is in charge of combining the measurements from multiple sensors, improving the reliability and fault tolerance of the integrated system, providing suitable auxiliary information for the ex- isting location determination system, and simplifying the system com- plexity over those track-side-relied solutions. Among different sensor fu- sion algorithms, Federated Kalman Filter (FKF) is a distributed cascaded integrated sensor fusion method and widely considered in applications because of its flexibility, low computational effort, and excellent fault tolerance capability. FKF consists of several local filters and a main filter, in which the system information is allocated in the local filters and then fused in the main filter. For the GNSS/INS/ODO integrated train posi- tioning system based on the FKF, INS is integrated due to its advantages in high output rate, autonomy and reliability. Considering the INS-related local filtering structures, two local filters for GNSS/INS and INS/ODO can 48 Fig. 1. Architecture of GNSS/INS/ODO sensor data fusion for integrated train positioning. be established, as shown in Fig. 1. High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 and the Doppler radar, also show great potentials in realizing the au- tonomous train positioning with a low cost over the Balise-based solu- tions [4]. However, it can be found that the single-sensor-based solution cannot satisfy the requirement of the train control system due to specific limitations and disadvantages in practical scenarios [5]. How to explore the capabilities of multiple perception sensors and break through the application-demand-oriented information fusion processing technology is the key to realize the satisfaction of novel train control systems with the train-centric design. Hence, it is important to overcome the key technologies of autonomous train positioning based on the fusion of GNSS and assistant sensors. By developing a novel train control system with dynamic configuration of the trains’ interval, reduced involvement of track-side equipments, and enhanced maintainability of the on-board train positioning system, the advantages of the train-centric mode will be practically reflected and utilized for specific railway lines. In this paper, key technologies of the novel train control system with GNSS- based autonomous perception are investigated and demonstrated in the practical environment, which illustrate the necessity and advantages of the utilization of GNSS, especially BDS (BeiDou Navigation Satellite System), in future safety-related railway systems in China. 2. Multi-sensor data fusion In existing railway signaling systems, the track occupancy of the train is determined by the track circuit, and the exact along-track lo- cation of the train is calculated by integrating both the train-borne odometer (or Doppler radar) and track-side Balises. The novel train- centric signaling system determines the track occupancy and train lo- cation without relying on those track-side facilities. Thus, raw mea- surements from multiple train-borne sensors will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. The main contributions of this paper are as follows. 1) A novel CPN-based modeling framework for GNSS-based train po- sitioning systems is developed. 2) Dynamic influences of signal interferences (i.e., by AM signals, FM signals, pulse signals) and environmental obstructions (i.e., by mountains and tunnels) are incorporated into the model. 3) The performance of the GNSS-based train positioning system is quantitatively evaluated under various scenarios with the CPN model of the system. 2. Related works Over the past decades, GNSS-based systems have been extensively ap- plied to train positioning due to their global coverage and high accuracy under favorable conditions [1,7]. Despite these advantages, challenges such as signal interferences and terrain-induced occlusions continue to pose questions on high positioning accuracy and stability. Numerous studies have explored techniques to mitigate the impacts of signal interferences on GNSS- based positioning accuracy [8–12]. For example, Ref. [9] investigates the impact of continuous wave interference on GNSS receiver tracking perfor- mance, examining the extent of its influence by varying data bit durations or integration times. To address interference-related challenges, an inter- ference energy detection method and a comprehensive orientation tech- nology tailored for GNSS are introduced in Ref. [11]. Besides, a number of researches have examined the impact of environmental factors along railway lines on GNSS signal integrity. For instance, Ref. [13] explores methods of scenario-based weighted measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. Some studies have achieved high positioning accuracy in partially obstructed or challenging environ- ments by fusing multi-sensor data [6,

content.abstract_or_snippet: positioning system should be sufficiently robust and accurate to cope with various complex environments and severe weather conditions. Generally, in autonomous driving systems, it is a common practice to increase the overall positioning and mapping performance by fusing two or more sensors. Reference [152] proposed a camera–radar sensor fusion framework based on vehicle component (rear corner) detection and positioning to improve the stability of vehicle positioning. The main idea of this method is to enhance the azimuth accuracy of radar information by detecting and locating the rear corner of the target vehicle from the image. The proposed method can effectively track the corner points of the vehicle using only one visible corner even in occluded scenes. GPS may experience signal attenuation in indoor spaces and urban canyons, and IMU may drift over time due to error accumulation when integrating acceleration to determine velocity and position. Reference [153] uses an unscented Kalman filter (UKF) Bayesian filter to fuse GPS and IMU data. Reference [154] uses the error state Kalman filter (ESKF) and the piecewise Rauch–Tung–Striebel (RTS) smoothing algorithm to fuse GNSS and IMU to improve the positioning accuracy and robustness of GNSS and IMU sensors. The proposed ESKF-RTS method provides more accurate and reliable positioning for autonomous vehicles, especially in environments where GNSS signals are unreliable. Reference [155] proposes a fusion algorithm that combines the Kalman filter (KF) with a new INS/GPS neural network framework (GI-NN) to assist INS in reducing the accumulated navigation error during GPS signal loss. Reference [156] studies the information fusion of GPS and INS sensors using the Kalman filter. In order to improve the performance of the Kalman filter, a robust method using the Mahalanobis distance is adopted. In order to World Electr. Veh. Reference [157] proposes an improved multi- sensor fusion positioning system based on GNSS/LiDAR/Vision/IMU. With semi-tight coupling and graph optimization. The system tightly couples the raw observation data of LiDAR, vision and IMU, while adding the positioning information of GNSS as a loosely coupled component to reduce the cumulative error. J. 2025, 16, 20 7 of 34 Although LiDAR is superior to other sensors in ranging accuracy and 3D perception, its performance is poor in harsh environments such as fog, snow, or rain. Heterogeneous sensor data fusion can eliminate information redundancy and loss, and provide reliable, stable, and efficient environmental perception capabilities, but the cost of the system will increase accordingly. 2.4. Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)/Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) The Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) and Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) are key components in autonomous driving systems, helping to determine the position, speed, and orientation of the vehicle. GNSS is a satellite-based navigation system that provides precise positioning and timing information anywhere on Earth. By triangulat- ing signals from multiple satellites, a GNSS receiver can calculate the vehicle’s position (latitude, longitude, and altitude) and speed. GNSS is widely used for global positioning and mapping, especially in open areas such as highways. In autonomous driving, it is one of the most commonly used vehicle positioning sensors. GNSS provides precise global positioning, which enables accurate positioning in open environments such as highways or rural roads. Differential GPS (DGPS), Real-Time Kinematic GPS (RTK-GPS), and Precise Point Positioning (PPP) technologies can be used to improve the accuracy of GNSS. The differential global positioning system (DGPS) operation consists of a reference station and a rover. Both stations use GPS receivers to receive positioning data from GPS satellites, use the positioning data collected by the reference station to calculate the positioning error, and transmit the error correction to the rover to improve the positioning accuracy [27]. RTK can achieve real-time centimeter-level positioning accuracy through double-difference ambigu- ity resolution (AR) [28]. Compared with differential techniques, precise point positioning (PPP) has a significant advantage in that it can accurately determine the position of a GNSS rover receiver by using external corrections from the Internet or dedicated correction satellites [29]. While both DGPS and RTK can achieve high positioning accuracy, GPS signal interruptions in urban environments and tunnels remain a challenge for these sensors. The IMU can provide information about the vehicle’s attitude, velocity, and direction. The IMU helps determine vehicle motion between GNSS updates and ensures

Virtual Balise The concept of VB (Virtual Balise) can effectively integrate the sa- tellite positioning technology into existing train control system frame- works without modification to the specifications [7,8]. VB capture is acknowledged by matching the train position and the preset VB re- ference position along the track [9]. Within a specific distance limit, a success matching will enable the determination of the time instant when a train passes through the target VB. Then, the VB telegram can be ex- tracted and transmitted to the ATP (Automatic Train Protection) system kernel. Based on the existing VB capture mechanism, a pre-capture de- cision logic is necessary to effectively reduce the risk of missed VB capturing. A VB capture recognition method based on the forward search strategy is proposed to accurately determine the position and time of VB capture, which improves the VB capture performance over conventional solution requires a pre-defined capture interval. 3.1. Pre-capture decision logic With the aid of the train state prediction method, the NVB (Next Virtual Balise) pre-capture decision is made according to the relative position relationship between the train and the NVB. For CBTC systems, the Centralized Traffic Control (CTC) of high- speed railway systems and the Automatic Train Supervision (ATS) of urban transit rail systems belong to the level 3 which is the operations management; the track circuit for high speed railway systems, axle counters of urban transit rail systems and Balise belong to the level 2 which is the supervisory control; The Vital Computer (VC) and Radio Block Center (RBC) for high speed railway systems and Vehicle on Board Computer (VOBC) and Zone Controller (ZC) for urban transit rail systems belong to the level 1 which is the basic or local control. Level 1, 2 and 3 belong to the cyber space. The level 0 which is the actual physical process belongs to the physical space that is the train in CBTC systems. From the reference model, the biggest difference between IT Fig. 2. The model of CBTC systems based on ISA-99 standards. 8 Z. Yu, H. Wang and F. Chen High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 6–17 security and ICS security is that ICS could control the physical entity and the malicious attacks could cause the physical entity destroyed which could lead to casualty and property losses. As shown in Fig. 2, the train is always running which means the requirements for CBTC train-ground communications are high. In the view of security, the train-ground communications should be resilient when some accidents happen. As a result, in CBTC systems, there are some backup designs for train- ground wireless communication. For urban rail transit systems, there are two radio channels allocated for control command transmission which is dual-net redundant. In addi- tion, if both of channels are blocked, the train-ground communications are realized through the axel counters and Balise. However, though the train is still running, the speed is smaller and the headway is larger which could decrease the efficiency and capacity of the overall subway line. For high-speed railway systems, the same mechanism is applied. When the wireless communication is interrupted, the track circuit and Balise are used to transmit the control command and the cost is the reduction of running speed from 350 km/h or 300 km/h to 250 km/h. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 175–184 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets Shuting Chena,b, Daohua Wua,b,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b National Research Center of Railway Safety Assessment, Collaborative Innovation Center of Railway Traffic Safety, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Jiang Liua,c,d, Siqi Wanga,d ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS-based train positioning Positioning performance Environment scenario GNSS signal interference Colored petri Nets Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-based continuous and accurate train positioning is one of the key technologies for advanced train operations such as train virtual coupling. However, GNSS-based train posi- tioning faces significant challenges in real-world scenarios due to environmental complexities and signal in- terferences. Considering this issue, this paper presents an approach for modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning systems using Colored Petri Nets (CPNs). By systematically modeling the GNSS signal reception and processing process, the performance of the positioning system under various environment scenarios is evaluated. The system model integrates three types of interference signals (i.e., Amplitude Modulation (AM) signals, Frequency Modulation (FM) signals, and pulse signals) while incorporating environ- mental factors such as terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding. Additionally, the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) algorithm is employed to process GNSS observation data, providing accurate train position estimations. The simulation results demonstrate that signal interferences and complex environmental conditions significantly affect the GNSS-based positioning accuracy. This study offers a comprehensive framework for evaluating the performance of GNSS-based train positioning systems in different scenarios, highlighting critical factors that influence positioning accuracy and stability. 1. Introduction With the increasing demand for operation safety and efficiency in rail transportation, train virtual coupling has been proposed and be- come a hot research topic in recent years. For virtual coupling

Safety stands for the ability to eliminate the risk of un- acceptable damage to a positioning unit. The railway sector uses safety integrity level to describe the likelihood that a system achieves the required safety function within a specified time under all specified conditions. The SIL specifies the safety integrity requirement for the safety functions assigned to the safety-related systems. Different SIL levels can be described in Table 5. The risk assessment needs to be performed for the safe design of train positioning architecture for the railway signaling applications. The risk assessment results can be classified as risky and the risk-free cases. Therefore, the output of safety assessment can be defined as the frequency of risky event Prisk. It has to be noted that the above-mentioned indices considering both the RAMS and GNSS systems are closely correlated, which means that performance evaluation is carried out based on a systemic measure. The relationship among the performance indicators for GNSS-based train positioning and state perception can be described as Fig. 8. 6. Field demonstration and analysis Based on the development of the novel train control system, an advanced train positioning unit for the railway train control applica- tion was designed and developed. The unit is realized based on the safe platform with a 2-out-of-2 (2oo2) redundant architecture. Under the safe platform architecture, the sensor data collection boards and communication boards are designed independently. Two sensor data collection boards are involved to independently collect raw measure- ments from the GPS/BDS receivers, INS sensors and the odometer. The communication boards are designed for information interaction be- tween the train positioning unit and the ATP kernel. The core position calculation unit realizes the sensor data fusion and location determi- nation using the presented technologies in this paper. In 2021, a field test was carried out at the Haergai-Muli railway. The test section is more than 120 kilometers with a high altitude between 3200 and 4200 m. Five stations are involved in the field test section, including Huancang, Chaidaer II, Chaidaer I, Wailihada and Jiangcang. Trackmap database of the test section was measured and generated before the field demonstration. content.abstract_or_snippet: measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. Some studies have achieved high positioning accuracy in partially obstructed or challenging environ- ments by fusing multi-sensor data [6,14,15]. In contrast to studies that mainly investigate GNSS signal inter- ferences in isolation, this paper integrates environmental factors and signal interferences into the formal model of the positioning system. By utilizing model simulation, the proposed approach enables a quantita- tive evaluation of the system’s performance. This method provides a systematic analysis and verification of the interactions among signal interferences, environmental conditions, and train positioning. Due to the rigorous mathematical foundation, formal methods have gained recognition as effective tools for system modeling and verification. In this work, we adopt CPNs [16] as the formal modeling framework to study the impact of signal interferences and environ- mental factors on GNSS-based train positioning. Petri nets are formal modeling languages that are well-known for systems characterized as concurrent, synchronous, distributed, etc. Some application examples adopting petri nets as the means of description for railway signaling systems can be found in Refs. [17–22]. 3. System modeling with CPNs Fig. 1 illustrates the modeling framework of a GNSS-based train positioning system comprising the following main parts: GNSS Re- ceiver, Environment Scenarios, Interference Signals, GNSS Position Solution, and Evaluation Module. The module GNSS Receiver selects specific environment scenarios and receives GNSS signals. The module Environment Scenarios is composed of three submodules: Open Area Scenario, Mountain Occlusion Scenario, and Tunnel and Exit Tunnel Scenario. These submodules simulate various real-world conditions while generating GNSS signal interferences, i.e., GNSS signals are interfered with by AM, FM, and pulse signals. In the module GNSS Positioning Solution, the EKF algorithm is employed to process interfered GNSS signals for estimating the train’s position. The module Evaluation compares the calculated position with the reference position and outputs the positioning error. Fig. 2 clearly illustrates the hierarchical relationships between modules of the CPN model, providing a structured view of their layered organization. The declarations of the model are categorized and summarized in two tables for clarity. Table 1 provides the colset declarations and their respective descriptions, while Table 2 provides the variable declara- tions and their respective descriptions. 3.1. This jamming can prevent the receiver from acquiring and maintaining a stable lock on the satellite signals, ultimately leading to degraded po- sitioning performance. 3.2. INS-aided jamming protection solution Fig. 2 illustrates the INS-aided GNSS tracking loop. The carrier signal measurement enters the tracking loop through the phase dis- criminator to generate a phase error estimate. This phase error drives the carrier loop filter which outputs an estimate of the signal Doppler to drive the carrier controller oscillator (NCO). The NCO duplicates the phase of the incoming signal, and this phase estimate is fed back to the phase discriminator. The tracking loop is usually said to be “locked” if the phase error is less than five degrees. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 information from both the INS and GNSS pseudorange/pseudorange rate, where a specific estimator (e.g., nonlinear Kalman filter) would be adopted. Based on this traditional tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integra- tion, the estimated navigation states are used to estimate the Doppler shift information, which is fed back to the GNSS tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The system operates in a closed-loop way where the integrated filter continuously updates the system’s state estimation by combining data from both the GNSS and INS, and the feedback of the estimated states helps improve the accuracy of both the INS and GNSS calculations. The INS-aided deep integration system leverages the in- ertial data, effectively narrowing the bandwidth of the tracking loop, which helps reduce the noise within the loop and suppress external jamming signals. The system’s capability to maintain accurate tracking and position estimation in jamming environments is significantly strengthened, leading to a more resilient navigation solution. 3. Jamming analysis and protection 3.1. Jamming analysis The received signal strength of the GNSS receiver is extremely low and falls well below the thermal noise floor. Typically, the received GPS signal levels fall within the range of −127 dBm [22]. The weak char- acteristics of GNSS signals will lead to an increasing risk of performance degradation under jamming conditions. Jamming signals may emanate from telecommunication and electronic systems operating in adjacent bands or in bands relatively far from the GNSS bands, i.e., Fre- quency Modulation (FM)/TV transmitter harmonics, Amplitude Mod- ulation (AM) transmitters, and mobile phone networks. In the simulated test, a GNSS signal at GPS L1 frequency is simulated with a reference level of −130 dBm, while the jamming generator operates at a center frequency of 1575.42 MHz with a power level of −90 dBm. The Jamming-to-Signal Ratio (JSR), i.e., the ratio of the jamming signal power to the GNSS signal power, is used to express the relationship between GNSS signal power and jamming power. Beyond complete signal obstruction scenarios, intermediate jamming power levels can induce significant performance degradation in GNSS re- ceivers without fully blocking satellite signal acquisition. These inter- mediate power levels would be hazardous and difficult to detect and mitigate, making it crucial to accurately assess the impact of jamming on GNSS signal quality. Previous research [18] has demonstrated sub- stantial signal quality degradation under jamming conditions with JSR values ranging from 38 to 54 dB. Accordingly, this study focuses on a 40 dB JSR scenario, corresponding to the specified −130 dBm GPS signal and −90 dBm jamming power configuration. A 5 Hz ramp waveform is employed, sweeping ± 1 kHz around the carrier. The jamming signal for investigating the collected IF signal, and the software-defined radio GNSS receiver are used to analyze the data. This software receiver is configured with a second-order phase-locked loop and a second-order code delay lock loop. content.abstract_or_snippet: performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con- tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter- ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly. Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex- periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance with a sweep frequency jamming scenario. 2. System design From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity, and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance, thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control systems. From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta- neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in- cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving the overall resilience of the navigation solution. Inertial mechanization is used to infer Inertial Solution at discrete epochs, while the GNSS tracking loop operates simultaneously until they meet the integration epoch. The setup of the GNSS jamming test platform is shown in Fig. 4, consisting of several key components, each connected through specific signal transmission paths, designed to enable jamming data collection for analytical processing. The Spirent GSS8000 GNSS simulator and the sight N5172B jamming signal generator are con- trolled from the simulator’s SimGEN host controller, which generates the required GPS L1 RF signals and the jamming signals, respectively. The jamming signals are defined, along with all the other scenario parameters, within the SimGEN. The scenario trajectory collected in the field is input into the SimGEN software, driving the simulator to generate the corre- sponding dynamic GNSS RF signals. A signal combiner is utilized to in- tegrate the clean GPS RF signal and the specific jamming signal. Since the down-converted Intermediate Frequency (IF) signal is what we need, the HG-softGPS06 GNSS IF signal collector is connected to the simulated signal to capture the necessary data, which is then collected and post-processed by the PC host. Field test results from a train location system were collected and used as the scenario script to drive the controller. A tactical-grade SPAN system (a high-precision GNSS/INS integrated system with a tactical grade, low noise IMU-FSAS) installed on the train was utilized to collect the tra- jectory and the IMU data. Two practical local trajectories that last for 160 s are adopted as the operation script to generate different GNSS jamming tested scenarios and drive the GNSS simulator. A similar signal interference set- ting is illustrated in Ref. [23]. Dynamic amplitude variations can sig- nificantly degrade GNSS signal reception, particularly in high-noise or contested environments. The transition FM Interference simulates frequency-modulated signal interference by generating two sinusoidal components with symmetrical frequency deviations around a carrier frequency centered at 1575.42 MHz, corresponding to the GNSS L1 band. These deviations are sampled from a Gaussian distribution with a standard deviation of 75 kHz, emulating the spectral characteristics of typical FM broadcast signals [24]. This approach captures the dynamic spectral spreading commonly observed in real-world RF interference. The transition Pulse Interference generates periodic pulse signals to si- mulate sudden and intense bursts of RF energy. It samples two random parameters: the pulse width Tp which represents the duration of each pulse, and the pulse interval Ti which defines the spacing between successive pulses. A uniformly distributed amplitude between 1 and 5 simulates the varying severity of the interference. At each time step, the model checks whether the current time modulo the total pulse period Tp + Ti falls within the pulse’s active duration. If so, an interference signal of the specified amplitude is emitted; otherwise, it remains zero. This periodic pulsed structure reflects typical burst-like interference patterns observed in GNSS environments [25], characterized by sharp, short-lived increases in signal strength followed by rapid attenuation. The input data enter the module through the input places Open Area and GNSS Signal, representing the GNSS satellites and the initial en- vironmental conditions. The place Bool Control and transition Mutex manage the logic for introducing signal interference, with the code in Mutex ensuring that the generation processes for different interference types do not conflict. Each interference signal is integrated into the GNSS data by firing the transition Interference. The GNSS data affected by signal interferences proceed to the observation output stage con- trolled by the place GNSS Number. The final results are outputted from the output place GNSS Observation. 3.2.2. Submodule Mountain Fig. 6 illustrates the shielding effect of mountainous terrain on GNSS signals. In addition to incorporating AM, FM, and pulse signal inter- ferences, the module introduces environmental factors of mountain height, terrain data, and obstruction distance. Jiang et al. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 It is of great importance to develop specific countermeasures to protect train positioning from jamming-caused GNSS disturbance. Research institutions and GNSS receiver manufacturers have been developing GNSS receivers equipped with anti-jamming capabilities. A survey of state-of-art approaches is introduced, based on the functional stages of the receiver in which they operate. Typically, they could be implemented at the front-end, pre-correction, or post-correlation stages, with others functioning at the navigation processing stage. Spatial fil- tering [9–11], functioning at the front-end stage, uses antenna arrays to point the receiver antenna beam toward the GNSS satellites and away from jammers. In the pre-correction stage, time/frequency filtering [12–14] enables the jamming mitigation before the jamming signal enters the receiver. Vector tracking is a typical approach that works on the post-correction stage. Instead of tracking in-view satellites sepa- rately in the conventional baseband signal processing, vector tracking [15,16] is achieved through feedback of the receiver states from the navigation module to the signal tracking channels as prior information and constraints. Additionally, the inertial-aiding-based strategy at the navigation processing stage provides a different way. INS is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning Zhuojian Caoa, Jiang Liua,b,c,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Wei Jianga,b,c, Baigen Caia,b,c ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS jamming Deeply-coupled integration GNSS/INS Railway safety and efficiency increasingly rely on precise train positioning. The integration of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) into railway control systems aims to reduce dependence on track-side in- frastructure. While GNSS has significantly improved train localization, challenges such as the susceptibility to jamming remain. To address this, this paper introduces an Inertial Navigation System (INS)-aided train posi- tioning system based on deep integration, exploring its performance through semi-physical experiments and simulations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed solution is able to reduce the positioning error by 63.47 %, and the velocity error by 58.47 % under jamming conditions. The study highlights the potential of deep integration for improving the resilience of GNSS-based train control systems, especially in the face of Radio Frequency (RF) jamming threats. 1. Introduction Railway safety and efficiency depend increasingly on knowing every train’s position. Traditional train positioning technologies usually in- volve track circuit positioning, axle counter positioning, and intersec- tion inductive loop positioning. However, these methods are often limited by insufficient positioning accuracy and high maintenance costs. The application of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) in railway train control systems aims to reduce as much as possible the use of track-side infrastructures for train location awareness, including track circuits and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. content.abstract_or_snippet: is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con- ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor- mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re- latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work. GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi- tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys- tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa- tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ- ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the anti- jamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-j

RAMS architecture for railway signaling The performance indices of GNSS-based positioning are different from the requirements of railway systems, especially for the safety-related railway applications. With the EN50126 standard definition, the RAMS (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability & Safety) architecture is presented with different requirements to specific railway systems and functions. 1) Reliability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform a specified function under specified conditions and within a specified time period. The evaluation indicators with respect to the reliability include failure rate, MTBF (Mean Time Between Failure) and MUT (Mean Up Time). 2) Availability. It refers to the extent to which a product or a system is in a workable or usable state at any moment when the required external re- sources are met or when it starts to perform its tasks. The availability of a product is a composite representation of its reliability and maintainability. 3) Maintainability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform specified maintenance work under the specified conditions and within the specified time using the specified procedures and resources for maintenance. 4) Safety. It refers to the ability to not incur risks that may cause damage. The safety requirements of the GNSS autonomous positioning- based train control system include functional safety requirements and other safety requirements. Among them, functional safety requirements are described by SIL (Safety Integrity Level), and different SIL levels are defined by the THR (Tolerable Hazard Rate). For a novel train control system based on autonomous perception using GNSS, the RAMS indices also have to be considered in the design and operation phases. The relationship of RAMS indices in the railway domain is shown in Fig. 7. If the reliability and maintainability are met, and long-term maintenance and control are performed according to the environment in which the system is located, the safety and availability of the system can also be met. 51 =zxgkkf[(),()](

content.abstract_or_snippet: . Safe train envelope estimation For GNSS-based train positioning and train integrity monitoring, the uncertainty in state estimation has to be concerned to ensure the safety and efficiency of train operation by utilizing the integrity monitoring result. Under the moving block train control scheme, where the occu- pancy of the track segments is determined by the practical length of the train, it is necessary to add a reasonable margin to the raw HOT and EOT location to form a safe occupancy range [13]. The safe train position has to cover the most unfavorable conditions. With this consideration, the margin should be sufficiently large to realize a high possibility to protect the unsatisfied situations. However, an excessive margin will lead to unreasonable identification of track occupancy, which may greatly af- fect the operational efficiency. To realize the expected balance level between the safety and efficiency characteristics, a constrained safe margin has to be adopted on the basis of safety and rationality, so as to reduce the train tracing interval and improve the system efficiency. The essence of the safe train envelope is to realize the safety as- surance of train control by describing the reliability of train positioning. The safe train envelope extends the positioning output based on the evaluation of the uncertainty of positioning errors, and provides a safe overlay to the most possible position derived by the HOT/EOT positioning unit. The safe margin consists of the HPL by integrity monitoring result and the confidential interval of the positioning error. The HPL, which indicates the radius of a circle in the horizontal plane with its center being at the true position to describe the region assured to contain the indicated horizontal position, is usually regarded as the safety boundary of positioning result by GNSS. However, the existence of the integrity risk may lead to an unbounded position error that exceeds the HPL. Without the high-level referencing system during the dynamic operation, it is difficult to accurately evaluate the relia- bility of the safety boundary by HPL. In order to guarantee the cred- ibility of the safe margin for envelope evaluation, it is necessary to estimate the possible range of the positioning error by a specific error confidence interval strategy. Considering the track-constrained along- track operation of railway trains, the error confidence interval indicates a one-dimensional along-track interval that can be represented by the uncertainties in state prediction, measurement update and the trackmap-based location determination, which means (4) and where confidence interval at instant k, result, geographic information from the trackmap database. represent the lower and upper limits of the indicates the user measurement represents the prior denotes the prediction result, and

Through the integration of static trackmap information, sensor data fusion for BDS/GPS re- ceivers, INS sensors and the odometer measurements were real- timely achieved with a high availability level under the complex and time-changing operation environment. Both the static and dynamic test cases were involved in the field demonstration. Table 6 sum- marizes the accuracy results of train positioning under different system modes. The results from field demonstration illustrate that involvement of the presented key technologies, including the safe platform and multi- source sensor fusion mechanism, effectively guarantees the perfor- mance of GNSS-based autonomous train positioning and state percep- tion even under complex and time-varying operational conditions. The utilization of the differential GNSS technology and the integrity mon- itoring logic makes it possible of achieving the high accuracy and ro- bustness against the possible difficult or even critical conditions. The field test results demonstrate the feasibility and the advantages of utilizing BDS in realizing a novel autonomous train control system with a train-centric design, which is of great significance in the future high- speed and plateau railway applications with the enhanced environ- mental flexibility and whole life cycle cost efficiency. 7. Conclusion The application of GNSS technology is one of the representative characteristics of intelligent railway systems in the future. However, there are specific requirements to the GNSS-based train positioning and state perception in the railway signaling applications due to the critical re- quirement to the safety. Thus, the utilization of GNSS has to be enhanced by the specific technical solutions. The sensor data fusion technique is of great significance to enhance the service availability under degraded or even a critical GNSS observing condition. To ensure the compatibility to the existing system specifications, the VB technology enables the in- tegration of the GNSS capabilities into the train control system and meanwhile the train integrity monitoring logic allows the opportunity to realize moving block-based train operation control through the dynamic identification of the track occupancy and safe envelope of the train. By investigating the relationship between the GNSS performance system and the RAMS specification, the performance evaluation of the GNSS-based train positioning can be effectively realized. It can be found that the in- volvement of these key technologies consolidates the autonomy and cap- abilities of the train-borne system, which releases the requirement to the track-side facilities like track circuit and Balises. content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train- centric railway signaling Baigen Caia,b,⁎ a School of Computer and Information Technology, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c School of Geodesy and Geomatics, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China d GNSS Research Center, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China e Space Engineering University, Beijing 101416, China , Jingnan Liuc,d, Xurong Donge, Jiang Liub ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: Railway signaling Train control Global Navigation Satellite System Sensor data fusion Virtual Balise Train integrity Performance evaluation The application of Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) in the intelligent railway systems is rapidly developing all over the world. With the GNSS-based train positioning and moving state perception, the au- tonomy and flexibility of a novel train control system can be greatly enhanced over the existing solutions relying on the track-side facilities. Considering the safety critical features of the railway signaling applications, the GNSS stand-alone mode may not be sufficient to satisfy the practical requirements. In this paper, the key technologies for applying GNSS in novel train-centric railway signaling systems are investigated, including the multi-sensor data fusion, Virtual Balise (VB) capturing and messaging, train integrity monitoring and system performance evaluation. According to the practical characteristics of the novel train control system under the moving block mode, the details of the key technologies are introduced. Field demonstration results of a novel train control system using the presented technologies under the practical railway operation conditions are presented to il- lustrate the achievable performance feature of autonomous train state perception using BeiDou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) and related solutions. It reveals the great potentials of these key technologies in the next generation train control system and other GNSS-based railway implementations. 1. Introduction The train control system plays a significant role in ensuring the safety of railway operation and improving the transportation efficiency. Chinese Train Control Systems (CTCSs), including CTCS level 2 and level 3, have been

content.abstract_or_snippet: iiiiLmatching=LSSmatchingHOTEOT B.G. Cai, J.N. Liu, X.R. Dong et al. High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 Fig. 6. Principle of length-based train integrity monitoring based on GNSS and trackmap. Due to the existence of positioning error, especially the uncertainty of EOT positioning resulted by the constrained satellite visibility, the uncertainty of the derived train length has to be concerned [12]. As shown in Fig. 6, an uncertainty buffer has to be added to the estimated HOT and EOT position to form an enhanced “envelope” for the train integrity determination purpose. A conservative evaluation of the un- certainty ranges is necessary due to the safety assurance target, and thus several factors related to the positioning performance have to be investigated, including the measurement noise, state estimation error and the potential fault(s) and failure(s). With this consideration, the derived train length would be larger than truth, and thus the threshold for train integrity identification has to be effectively determined to avoid false alarm to the abnormal train integrity events. The uncertainties in HOT and EOT positioning results lead to the es- timation deviation of the train length. When the quality of GNSS posi- tioning degrades with the change of the observation condition, the de- viation of train length estimation would be large and thus the failure of accurate train integrity monitoring may occur. Therefore, the consistency evaluation of the HOT/EOT relative speed can be involved to consolidate the integrity status identification through multiple information resources. Considering the errors in position and speed measurements of the both ends, it would be necessary to constrain the errors through the filtering operation. It can be found that there are mainly two specific trends to the monitoring variables, including relative static and relative acceleration according to the HOT-EOT-based train length and relative speed estimation. Therefore, a sliding window strategy can be involved for mean filtering, and then the filtered data in the window can be fitted by LS-based linear fitting. According to the fitting results, the qualita- tive trend of the data in the sliding window can be recognized to ana- lyze the changing status of the HOT/EOT relative speed and the esti- mation deviation of the train length. 4.2. Safe train envelope estimation For GNSS-based train positioning and train integrity monitoring, the uncertainty in state estimation has to be concerned to ensure the safety and efficiency of train operation by utilizing the integrity monitoring result.
